{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional network
  perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
  default — even if already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect information assets. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from military
  strategy and has been adapted to information security.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize based on all available data
    points (user identity, device health, location, service, classification).
  • Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum access necessary, and only
    for the duration required (Just-In-Time / Just-Enough-Access).
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised. Segment
    access, encrypt everywhere, and use continuous monitoring and analytics.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones; lateral
    movement is blocked by default.
  • Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent; it is constantly
    re-evaluated for every access request.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Controls — Implement security at multiple levels: physical,
    network, host, application, and data.
  • Redundancy — No single point of failure; overlapping controls compensate
    for each other's weaknesses.
  • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter Focus (Traditional) — Historically emphasizes a strong outer
    boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
  • Assume Failure — Each layer is designed with the expectation that outer
    layers may be breached.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ASPECT              | ZERO TRUST                      | DEFENSE IN DEPTH               |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Philosophy          | Trust nothing, verify everything| Layer defenses so one failure  |
|                     | continuously.                    | doesn't compromise all.        |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Network Assumption  | The network is always hostile;  | The perimeter can be defended; |
|                     | no implicit trust from location. | internal traffic is safer.     |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Primary Focus       | Identity, data, and access      | Infrastructure and network     |
|                     | control at the resource level.  | topology.                      |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Access Model        | Per-session, per-resource,      | Often broader network-based    |
|                     | continuously verified.          | access once inside perimeter.  |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement    | Blocked by default via micro-   | Slowed by internal layers but  |
|                     | segmentation.                   | often still possible.          |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Trust Model         | Trust is a vulnerability;       | Trust is earned at the         |
|                     | eliminate it entirely.          | perimeter and maintained.      |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Architecture        | Identity-centric, cloud-native, | Network-centric, often tied to |
|                     | works across any environment.   | physical/datacenter topology.  |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Historical Context  | Modern response to cloud,       | Traditional military-derived   |
|                     | mobile, and remote work.        | approach to fortification.     |


4. COMPLEMENTARY NATURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These two approaches are NOT mutually exclusive. In fact, they are highly
complementary:

  • Zero Trust can be seen as a specific implementation strategy WITHIN a
    broader Defense in Depth framework. Zero Trust provides the access control
    and identity layer, while Defense in Depth ensures that physical security,
    endpoint protection, data encryption, and other layers are also in place.

  • Defense in Depth without Zero Trust may still rely too heavily on

{"content": "================================================================================
  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional network
  perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
  default — even if already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect information assets. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from military
  strategy and has been adapted to information security.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize based on all available data
    points (user identity, device health, location, service, classification).
  • Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum access necessary, and only
    for the duration required (Just-In-Time / Just-Enough-Access).
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised. Segment
    access, encrypt everywhere, and use continuous monitoring and analytics.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones; lateral
    movement is blocked by default.
  • Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent; it is constantly
    re-evaluated for every access request.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Controls — Implement security at multiple levels: physical,
    network, host, application, and data.
  • Redundancy — No single point of failure; overlapping controls compensate
    for each other's weaknesses.
  • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter Focus (Traditional) — Historically emphasizes a strong outer
    boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
  • Assume Failure — Each layer is designed with the expectation that outer
    layers may be breached.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ASPECT              | ZERO TRUST                      | DEFENSE IN DEPTH               |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Philosophy          | Trust nothing, verify everything| Layer defenses so one failure  |
|                     | continuously.                    | doesn't compromise all.        |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Network Assumption  | The network is always hostile;  | The perimeter can be defended; |
|                     | no implicit trust from location. | internal traffic is safer.     |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Primary Focus       | Identity, data, and access      | Infrastructure and network     |
|                     | control at the resource level.  | topology.                      |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Access Model        | Per-session, per-resource,      | Often broader network-based    |
|                     | continuously verified.          | access once inside perimeter.  |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement    | Blocked by default via micro-   | Slowed by internal layers but  |
|                     | segmentation.                   | often still possible.          |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Trust Model         | Trust is a vulnerability;       | Trust is earned at the         |
|                     | eliminate it entirely.          | perimeter and maintained.      |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Architecture        | Identity-centric, cloud-native, | Network-centric, often tied to |
|                     | works across any environment.   | physical/datacenter topology.  |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Historical Context  | Modern response to cloud,       | Traditional military-derived   |
|                     | mobile, and remote work.        | approach to fortification.     |


4. COMPLEMENTARY NATURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These two approaches are NOT mutually exclusive. In fact, they are highly
complementary:

  • Zero Trust can be seen as a specific implementation strategy WITHIN a
    broader Defense in Depth framework. Zero Trust provides the access control
    and identity layer, while Defense in Depth ensures that physical security,
    endpoint protection, data encryption, and other layers are also in place.

  • Defense in Depth without Zero Trust may still rely too heavily on the
    perimeter and implicitly trust internal traffic — a weakness that modern
    attackers exploit through phishing, insider threats, and credential theft.

  • Zero Trust without Defense in Depth may overlook physical security,
    hardware supply chain risks, and other non-digital attack

  • Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize based on all available data
    points (user identity, device health, location, service, classification).
  • Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum access necessary, and only
    for the duration required (Just-In-Time / Just-Enough-Access).
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised. Segment
    access, encrypt everywhere, and use continuous monitoring and analytics.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones; lateral
    movement is blocked by default.
  • Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent; it is constantly
    re-evaluated for every access request.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Controls — Implement security at multiple levels: physical,
    network, host, application, and data.
  • Redundancy — No single point of failure; overlapping controls compensate
    for each other's weaknesses.
  • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter Focus (Traditional) — Historically emphasizes a strong outer
    boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
  • Assume Failure — Each layer is designed with the expectation that outer
    layers may be breached.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ASPECT              | ZERO TRUST                      | DEFENSE IN DEPTH               |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Philosophy          | Trust nothing, verify everything| Layer defenses so one failure  |
|                     | continuously.                    | doesn't compromise all.        |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Network Assumption  | The network is always hostile;  | The perimeter can be defended; |
|                     | no implicit trust from location. | internal traffic is safer.     |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Primary Focus       | Identity, data, and access      | Infrastructure and network     |
|                     | control at the resource level.  | topology.                      |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Access Model        | Per-session, per-resource,      | Often broader network-based    |
|                     | continuously verified.          | access once inside perimeter.  |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement    | Blocked by default via micro-   | Slowed by internal layers but  |
|                     | segmentation.                   | often still possible.          |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Trust Model         | Trust is a vulnerability;       | Trust is earned at the         |
|                     | eliminate it entirely.          | perimeter and maintained.      |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Architecture        | Identity-centric, cloud-native, | Network-centric, often tied to |
|                     | works across any environment.   | physical/datacenter topology.  |
|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Historical Context  | Modern response to cloud,       | Traditional military-derived   |
|                     | mobile, and remote work.        | approach to fortification.     |


4. COMPLEMENTARY NATURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

These two approaches are NOT mutually exclusive. In fact, they are highly
complementary:

  • Zero Trust can be seen as a specific implementation strategy WITHIN a
    broader Defense in Depth framework. Zero Trust provides the access control
    and identity layer, while Defense in Depth ensures that physical security,
    endpoint protection, data encryption, and other layers are also in place.

  • Defense in Depth without Zero Trust may still rely too heavily on the
    perimeter and implicitly trust internal traffic — a

  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional network
  perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application should be trusted by
  default — even if already inside the network.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect information assets. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from military
  strategy and has been adapted to information security.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize based on all available data
    points (user identity, device health, location, service, classification).
  • Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum access necessary, and only
    for the duration required (Just-In-Time / Just-Enough-Access).
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised. Segment
    access, encrypt everywhere, and use continuous monitoring and analytics.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones; lateral
    movement is blocked by default.
  • Continuous Verification — Trust is never permanent; it is constantly
    re-evaluated for every access request.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Controls — Implement security at multiple levels: physical,
    network, host, application, and data.
  • Redundancy — No single point of failure; overlapping controls compensate
    for each other's weaknesses.
  • Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter Focus (Traditional) — Historically emphasizes a strong outer
    boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
  • Assume Failure — Each layer is designed with the expectation that outer
    layers may be breached.
